importer_demand_or_import_share: 33.4% of 2024 Hormuz crude destination volume; compare cautiously with EIA 2024 China crude imports of 11.1 mb/d
substitution_notes: Can seek Russia, Atlantic Basin, and sanctioned/indirect barrels, but refinery quality, freight, sanctions risk, and longer voyages limit immediate substitution.
caveats: Destination volume is EIA/Vortexa route estimate, not customs imports by origin; condensate/crude definitions may not match national import statistics.

importer_demand_or_import_share: 13.2% of 2024 Hormuz crude destination volume
substitution_notes: Can draw more Russian, US, African, and Latin American barrels, but delivered cost and refinery slate constraints matter; Russian discount/sanctions dynamics are separate from physical Hormuz exposure.
caveats: Destination volume is EIA/Vortexa route estimate; PPAC is the primary source for India import totals but this row does not harmonize fiscal-year Indian import totals.

importer_demand_or_import_share: 12.1% of 2024 Hormuz crude destination volume; secondary crisis estimates put Middle East crude reliance around 70%
substitution_notes: Can increase US and non-Middle-East barrels, but distance, grades, term contracts, and refinery configuration limit quick replacement.
caveats: EIA route volume is high quality; importer reliance percentage is from secondary analysis and should be checked against Korea customs before publication.

importer_demand_or_import_share: 10.6% of 2024 Hormuz crude destination volume; Japan official material says Middle East supplies more than 90% of crude imports
substitution_notes: Japan has strategic/commercial oil stocks and can seek Atlantic Basin cargoes, but structural Middle East reliance and long-haul replacement make exposure high.
caveats: Japan official dependence figure is FY2022 brochure evidence, not a 2024 customs calculation; EIA destination volume is 2024 route estimate.

importer_demand_or_import_share: 14.5% of 2024 Hormuz crude destination volume
substitution_notes: Likely includes Southeast Asian refiners and trading hubs; substitutes depend on country-level refinery slates and inventories.
caveats: EIA figure does not break out countries inside Other Asia; avoid treating this as one demand system.

importer_demand_or_import_share: 5.0% of 2024 Hormuz crude destination volume
direct_exposure_category: direct moderate importer exposure plus global price exposure
substitution_notes: Europe can substitute more readily than Asia through Atlantic Basin, North Sea, West Africa, and US barrels, but price exposure remains global.
caveats: Direct physical exposure is smaller than Asia; this row excludes refined products and LNG spillovers.

importer_demand_or_import_share: EIA article: about 7% of US crude/condensate imports and 2% of US petroleum liquids consumption
direct_exposure_category: direct low importer exposure; high global price exposure
substitution_notes: US can lean on domestic production, Canada, Latin America, and strategic/commercial stocks; US consumers still face global price pass-through.
caveats: US row is unusually well specified by EIA; does not include indirect exposure through global product prices.

importer_demand_or_import_share: 7.9% of 2024 Hormuz crude destination volume
substitution_notes: Unknown destinations may include trading hubs or unclassified cargoes; substitution cannot be assessed without cargo-level data.
caveats: Computed as Saudi Arabia plus Other destination rows in EIA figure data; not a clean end-user importer category.

importer_demand_or_import_share: IEA: almost 90% of Hormuz LNG exports; more than one-quarter of Asia LNG imports
substitution_notes: No practical route bypass for Qatar/UAE LNG. Substitution relies on spot LNG from US/Australia/others, storage draw, fuel switching, and demand response.
caveats: Baseline volume approximates 90% of IEA's over-110 bcm Hormuz LNG figure; not a country-level destination split.

importer_demand_or_import_share: IEA: just over 10% of Hormuz LNG exports
direct_exposure_category: direct moderate LNG exposure plus global price exposure
substitution_notes: Europe has storage, pipeline imports, US LNG, and demand response, but competes in the same global LNG market as Asia.
caveats: Baseline volume approximates 10-11% of IEA's over-110 bcm Hormuz LNG figure; country-level Europe split not public in this matrix.

importer_demand_or_import_share: IEA: about 80% of oil and oil products transiting Hormuz in 2025 was destined for Asia; product-specific Asia share not separately public
substitution_notes: Can substitute from Asian refiners, Atlantic Basin products, stock draws, and demand restraint, but diesel/jet/LPG/naphtha availability and freight constraints differ by product.
caveats: 3.3 mb/d is IEA Gulf refined-products scale, not an exact Asia-only destination volume; avoid summing with crude or LPG rows.

importer_demand_or_import_share: IEA: almost all Middle East LPG exported in 2025 went to Asia; 30% of all seaborne LPG exports transited Hormuz
substitution_notes: Replacement cargoes can come from the US or elsewhere but voyage times are much longer; LPG storage is limited in many importers.
caveats: IEA baseline is all Hormuz LPG exports, not a country-level destination table; overlaps partly with petroleum product rows.

unit: mb/d disrupted in first two months
importer_demand_or_import_share: IEA: around two-thirds of LPG consumed in India transited Hormuz in 2025; imports dropped by more than half early in the conflict
direct_exposure_category: direct household and commercial fuel exposure
substitution_notes: India ordered refineries to maximize LPG output and sought alternative cargoes, but US-to-Mumbai voyage time is about 40 days versus 4-5 days from Hormuz; storage covers just over 10 days consumption.
caveats: Baseline_volume is an observed disruption estimate, not normal import volume; kept because IEA gives unusually specific India exposure.

exporter_or_origin_region: Saudi Arabia Qatar Bahrain and other upstream Hormuz producers
unit: Mt/year exporter baseline in repo rows
importer_demand_or_import_share: IFASTAT: upstream Hormuz countries account for 34% of global urea trade; IFA says impacts are largest in South Asia and Latin America
substitution_notes: Substitution can come from Russia, China if export policy allows, North Africa, and domestic production, but planting-season timing and gas/feedstock costs constrain replacement.
caveats: Exporter baseline sums Saudi urea, Qatar production/export proxy, and Bahrain urea rows from repo estimates; not a bilateral importer volume.

importer_or_region: South Asia Morocco and South Korea
unit: Mt/year exporter baseline in repo rows
importer_demand_or_import_share: IFASTAT: upstream Hormuz countries account for 23% of global ammonia trade; importer demand share not public in current matrix
direct_exposure_category: direct fertilizer and industrial feedstock exposure
substitution_notes: Ammonia can be imported directly, replaced by domestic production where gas is available, or embodied in urea/phosphate flows; double-counting with urea is a major risk.
caveats: Exporter baseline includes Qatar production/export proxy and should not be added to urea exposure as independent final demand.

exporter_or_origin_region: Upstream Hormuz producers and sulfur/ammonia feedstock chains
importer_demand_or_import_share: IFASTAT: upstream Hormuz countries account for 18% of global MAP+DAP trade
direct_exposure_category: direct phosphate fertilizer exposure plus indirect sulfur/ammonia feedstock exposure
substitution_notes: Alternative phosphate fertilizer supply depends on sulfuric acid, ammonia, export controls, and producer inventories; price response can exceed direct Hormuz-origin DAP/MAP volume.
caveats: Matrix records market share rather than bilateral volume; exact importer exposure needs customs data.

exporter_or_origin_region: Qatar UAE Saudi and other Gulf suppliers
importer_demand_or_import_share: NDSU secondary analysis: China is the world's largest sulfur importer; 46% sourced from the Middle East; about 1.8 MMT port inventory in late February
direct_exposure_category: direct feedstock exposure for phosphate and sulfuric acid chains
substitution_notes: China can draw port inventories and ration/export-restrict phosphate products, but Gulf sulfur loss constrains phosphate output and acid availability.
caveats: China sulfur numbers are from NDSU using trade/industry data, not an official Chinese source; IEA/IFA provide the global Hormuz sulfur share.

exporter_or_origin_region: Qatar UAE Saudi and other Gulf suppliers
importer_demand_or_import_share: NDSU secondary analysis: Morocco's OCP depends on roughly 3.7 MMT Gulf sulfur
direct_exposure_category: direct feedstock exposure for global phosphate exports
substitution_notes: Morocco has phosphate rock but needs imported sulfur for sulfuric acid; substitution requires alternate sulfur supply and shipping capacity.
caveats: Secondary-source exposure estimate; verify against OCP/import customs before final publication.

exporter_or_origin_region: Qatar UAE Saudi and other Gulf suppliers
importer_demand_or_import_share: NDSU secondary analysis: Indonesia sources about 60% of sulfur from the Middle East
substitution_notes: Alternative sulfur supply may exist, but concentration and freight delays matter for phosphate/industrial users.
caveats: NDSU reports share but not a public tonnage in the excerpted source; keep as qualitative until customs data are pulled.

importer_demand_or_import_share: IEA: Gulf region produces around 8% of global aluminium supply; about 5 Mt/year shipped through Hormuz
substitution_notes: Aluminium is globally traded and recyclable, but primary metal replacement is constrained by power-intensive smelter capacity and long supply chains.
caveats: Importer split not public in current matrix; treat as global industrial exposure until UN Comtrade destination rows are reconciled.

importer_demand_or_import_share: IEA: remainder of Hormuz LPG exports beyond cooking use was mostly water heating and petrochemical feedstock, with petrochemical use dominated by China
direct_exposure_category: direct feedstock and downstream plastics exposure
substitution_notes: China has some flexibility to switch feedstocks when economical, but feedstock scarcity and naphtha/LPG price spikes can curtail polymer production.
caveats: No clean public bilateral feedstock matrix yet; use as mechanism row, not a standalone volume estimate.
